<div class="logo">
  <img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="SafeNex Logo"/>
</div>

# SafeNex: Technical Documentation (Software MVP)

## 1. Introduction

### What is SafeNex?
SafeNex is a modern, software-based safety platform designed to provide real-time location tracking, emergency alerts, and intelligent behavior monitoring. It aims to offer peace of mind to guardians by connecting a user's mobile device directly to a secure, centralized dashboard.

### Problem Statement
Personal safety remains a significant concern, especially for vulnerable individuals in unpredictable environments. Traditional tracking methods often lack real-time capabilities or proactive intelligence. There is a need for a unified platform that not only tracks location but also identifies unusual behavior and provides immediate emergency escalation.

### Objectives of the Software MVP
The primary objective of this Minimum Viable Product (MVP) is to establish a robust software foundation. This includes:
- Real-time GPS location tracking via a mobile app.
- Instant SOS emergency triggering.
- A centralized admin/guardian dashboard for live monitoring.
- A secure, scalable backend architecture to process data.

### Scope
- **Included**: Mobile application (Flutter), backend REST API (FastAPI), modern web dashboard (Next.js), database (Supabase PostgreSQL), containerized deployment (Docker), and purely software-based AI anomaly detection.
- **Excluded**: Custom hardware integration (e.g., ESP32, wearables), cloud-only deployment dependencies (the system runs fully locally), and external third-party AI APIs (the AI logic runs securely within the backend itself).

## 2. System Overview

SafeNex consists of four interconnected components running together in a unified environment. 

- **Flutter Mobile Application**: The user-facing mobile app installed on smartphones. It gathers GPS data, provides the SOS button, and sends this data securely to the backend.
- **Backend Server**: Built with Python and FastAPI, this acts as the "brain." It receives data from the mobile app, runs AI logic to detect anomalies, and communicates with the database.
- **Database**: We use Supabase, which provides a fast PostgreSQL database along with built-in authentication and real-time data streaming capabilities.
- **Admin Panel**: A web interface built with Next.js where guardians or administrators can visually track users on a live map and view historical data or alerts.
- **AI Logic**: An internal Python module in the backend that analyzes incoming location and activity data to flag unusual patterns (like moving too fast).
- **Docker Environment**: All of the above (excluding the mobile app installed on a phone) runs inside isolated "containers" using Docker, ensuring that the system works exactly the same on any computer.

**How They Communicate**:
The Mobile App sends HTTP requests to the Backend Server. The Backend processes these requests and stores the data in the Supabase Database. The Admin Panel connects to the Database to fetch and display this data in real-time.

## 3. Technology Stack

### Flutter
Used for the mobile application. Flutter allows us to write code once and deploy it to both Android and iOS devices. It is highly performant and offers great support for background location tracking.

### FastAPI (Python)
Used for the backend server. FastAPI is incredibly fast, modern, and automatically generates API documentation. Python is chosen because it easily supports data analysis and AI logic directly within the server.

### Supabase (Auth + PostgreSQL + Realtime)
Used as the database and authentication provider. Supabase provides a powerful PostgreSQL database, handles user logins securely (Auth), and allows the admin panel to listen for instant updates (Realtime) without complex web-socket setups.

### Next.js (React)
Used for the web-based Admin Panel. Next.js is a React framework that makes building fast, interactive dashboards easy and provides excellent performance and routing out of the box.

### Docker & Docker Compose
Used to package the backend and web dashboard into standardized containers. This guarantees that "if it works on my machine, it will work on yours" without messy local installations of different software.

### Python AI Libraries
Standard Python mathematical and data structures are used to calculate distances, speeds, and flag anomalies based on the location telemetry.

### Mapping Libraries
**Leaflet / React-Leaflet** are used in the Next.js admin panel to render beautiful, interactive live maps securely.

## 4. Prerequisites & Installation Tools

Before running SafeNex locally, you must install the following tools on your machine.

### Operating System Requirements
SafeNex is compatible with Windows (via WSL2), macOS, and Linux.

### Git
Used to download the project source code.
- **Verify**: Open terminal and type `git --version`.

### Docker & Docker Compose
Required to run the backend and admin panel without complex local configuration.
- **Verify**: Type `docker --version` and `docker-compose --version`. Ensure the Docker Desktop engine is running.

### Node.js & npm
Required if you wish to run or modify the Next.js web projects outside of Docker. 
- **Verify**: Type `node -v` and `npm -v`.

### Flutter SDK
Required to build and run the mobile application.
- **Verify**: Type `flutter doctor`.

### Android Studio / Emulator
Required to simulate an Android phone on your computer to test the Flutter app.
- **Verify**: Ensure you have an Android Virtual Device (AVD) set up and running via Android Studio.

### Python 3.10+
Required if you intend to run the backend script directly without Docker (though Docker is recommended).
- **Verify**: Type `python --version` or `python3 --version`.

## 5. Project Folder Structure

The SafeNex repository is divided into distinct folders, separating concerns perfectly.

```
/Safenex
├── safenex-backend/      # The FastAPI python server
├── safenex-mobile/       # The Flutter Android/iOS application
├── safenex-web/          # The public landing page 
├── safenex-admin/        # The Guardian/Admin dashboard
├── docker-compose.yml    # The master file to run the web and backend
├── supabase/             # Database initialization scripts
```

- **`safenex-backend/`**: Contains Python routing, database connection logic, and the internal AI validation scripts.
- **`safenex-mobile/`**: Contains Dart code, screen layouts (Login, Dashboard, Profile), and the geolocator logic.
- **`safenex-admin/`**: Contains React components, the LiveMap implementation, and real-time subscription hooks.
- **`docker-compose.yml`**: Defines how the backend, admin, and web projects boot up simultaneously and talk to each other.

## 6. Database Design (Supabase)

### Supabase Overview
Supabase is an open-source alternative to Firebase. We use it instead of raw PostgreSQL because it provides a ready-to-use REST API, built-in Authentication, and Realtime websocket listeners, saving weeks of development time.

### Authentication & RLS
Supabase handles user accounts securely. It uses **Row Level Security (RLS)**—PostgreSQL rules that ensure a user can only read or write their *own* data. In our MVP, we provide bypass scripts to disable RLS strictly for local, frictionless testing.

### Key Tables
1. **`users`**: Stores user profiles.
   - *Columns*: `id`, `email`, `full_name`, `role` (parent, child, admin).
2. **`relationships`**: Links parents to specific children.
   - *Columns*: `parent_id`, `child_id`.
3. **`locations`**: Stores every GPS ping from the mobile app.
   - *Columns*: `id`, `user_id`, `latitude`, `longitude`, `timestamp`.
4. **`geofences`**: Defines safe zones.
   - *Columns*: `id`, `user_id`, `radius`, `center_lat`, `center_lng`.
5. **`sos_alerts`**: Tracks emergency button presses.
   - *Columns*: `id`, `user_id`, `status` (active/resolved), `timestamp`.
6. **`activity_logs`**: General logs (e.g., login, tracking started).
   - *Columns*: `id`, `user_id`, `action`, `created_at`.
7. **`ai_insights`**: Stores anomalies flagged by the Python backend.
   - *Columns*: `id`, `user_id`, `anomaly_type`, `score`, `created_at`.
8. **`daily_summaries`**: AI-generated reports combining the day's activity.
   - *Columns*: `id`, `user_id`, `summary_text`, `risk_level`, `date`.

## 7. Authentication & Authorization

### Email & Password Login
Users log into the system using their email and password. In our local MVP, we use hardcoded or simple test scripts to pair users easily without setting up SMTP email servers.

### JWT Token Flow
When a user logs in, the backend verifies their credentials and issues a JSON Web Token (JWT). The Flutter app saves this token securely and sends it in the `Authorization` header with every subsequent request.

### Role-Based Access
Users are assigned roles (`admin`, `parent`, or `child`). The frontend and database structure use these roles to decide rendering (e.g., a child cannot view all users on the admin map).

### Secure Storage in Flutter
Tokens and user IDs are stored using Flutter's `shared_preferences` (or secure storage packages) ensuring that the user remains logged in even if the app restarts.

## 8. Flutter Mobile Application

### App Startup Flow
1. **Splash Screen**: Displays the SafeNex logo while the app initializes.
2. **Login Screen**: Prompts for credentials. Upon success, navigates to the Dashboard.
3. **Dashboard / Profile**: The main tracking hub and user settings area.

### How Location Tracking Works
When the user presses "Start Tracking," the Flutter app requests GPS permissions. It then uses the `geolocator` package to grab high-accuracy latitude and longitude coordinates. A timer fires (e.g., every 15 seconds) to send an HTTP POST request to the backend with the current coordinates.

### SOS Button Functionality
Tapping the SOS button grabs an immediate, highly-accurate GPS fix and sends an emergency HTTP POST payload to the server. The server immediately registers an `active` SOS alert.

### Environment Variables
The mobile app uses `http://10.0.2.2:8000` as the backend base URL. `10.0.2.2` is a special IP that allows an Android emulator to access the host computer's `localhost`.

## 9. Maps & Location Tracking

### GPS Data Collection
The mobile app reads device hardware GPS. This is sent to the FastAPI backend, which validates the payload and instantly writes it to the `locations` table in Supabase.

### Displaying on the Map
The Admin Panel uses `react-leaflet`. It queries the `locations` table on load to get the latest coordinates of all users. Furthermore, it subscribes to Supabase Realtime; when a new location row is inserted, the map instantly updates the marker position without requiring a page refresh.

### Empty Maps
If the map appears empty, it is usually because the Flutter app has not sent any data yet, or the emulator has not been provided with mock GPS coordinates via Android Studio's extended controls.

## 10. Parent–Child Relationship Logic

### How Accounts Link
The `relationships` table maps a `parent_id` to a `child_id`. This creates a hierarchy in the database.

### Access Restrictions
Through backend logic (and database RLS in production), queries are structured so a parent can only retrieve location data where the `user_id` matches their linked children's IDs. A child account logging in has an interface dedicated solely to sending their own location and viewing their own status—they cannot query the location of other users.

## 11. Geo-Fencing (Conceptual)

### What it is
Geo-fencing allows a guardian to draw an invisible circular boundary on a map.

### How it Works Logically
The backend stores a center latitude/longitude and a radius in meters. When the backend receives a new location ping from a user, it calculates the distance between the user's location and the geo-fence center using the Haversine formula. If the distance exceeds the radius, an alert is generated.

### MVP Implementation
While the `geofences` table exists and the backend contains logic to evaluate breaches, the actual UI to draw these fences on the map is considered an extension. In the current MVP, you can manually insert a fence into the database to test the backend trigger logic.

## 12. SOS Emergency System

### Flow
User taps "SOS Alert" -> Flutter App sends high-priority request -> Backend creates row in `sos_alerts` -> Admin Dashboard Realtime listener detects new row -> UI flashes red and updates the "Active SOS" count.

### Data Sent
The payload includes `user_id`, Exact GPS `latitude` & `longitude`, and the exact `timestamp` of the emergency.

### Storage & Real-time
Stored permanently in the database for auditing. The Next.js dashboard uses Supabase channels to listen for `INSERT` events on the `sos_alerts` table, popping up a priority banner instantly.

## 13. AI Module (Software-Level Only)

### Purpose
To convert raw GPS numbers into human-understandable behavior and detect unseen risks.

### What it Analyzes
It calculates the speed and distance between consecutive location updates.

### When it Runs
The AI evaluation can run synchronously on every location update or asynchronously through a background cron job summarizing the day's activity.

### Anomaly Triggers & Missing Data
If the calculated speed is impossibly fast (e.g., crossing a city in 2 seconds), it flags an `anomaly_type: "impossible_speed"`. 
**Note**: AI logic requires variance in data. If you test the app by sitting still, no anomalies will be generated. You must simulate movement in the Android emulator to trigger these flags.

## 14. Admin Panel (Web)

### Purpose
A central command center for guardians or institutional administrators.

### Features
It provides a Live Map of all connected users, a live feed of activity logs (who connected, who moved), a list of active hardware devices, and a summary of AI-generated behavior alerts.

### Roles
In the MVP, the dashboard provides a god-view of the database for demonstration purposes. In production, login logic strictly filters this view exclusively to the users tied to the logged-in administrator.

## 15. Backend Architecture (FastAPI)

### API Structure
FastAPI handles incoming HTTP requests. It uses Pydantic models to strictly validate the data sent by the mobile app (e.g., ensuring latitude is a valid floating-point number).

### Route Setup
Endpoints are organized. For instance, `/api/v1/locations/update` handles GPS, and `/api/v1/sos/trigger` handles emergencies.

### Background Tasks
FastAPI allows for "background tasks." When an SOS is triggered, the response is sent immediately to the mobile app, while the heavy lifting of logging the alert and generating side-effects happens in the background.

## 16. Dockerization & Local Deployment

### Why Docker?
Docker ensures the backend and frontend run securely in identical environments on any machine, eliminating "missing dependency" errors.

### docker-compose.yml
This file orchestrates everything. It tells Docker to build the `safenex-backend` image, the `safenex-web` image, and the `safenex-admin` image simultaneously and assigns them specific accessible ports.

### Port Mappings
- **Web Landing Page**: `http://localhost:3000`
- **Admin Panel**: `http://localhost:3001`
- **FastAPI Backend**: `http://localhost:8000`

### Step-by-Step Run Commands
1. Open a terminal in the root SafeNex project folder.
2. Build and start containers: 
   `docker-compose up -d --build`
3. View logs to debug: 
   `docker-compose logs -f`
4. Stop the containers: 
   `docker-compose down`

## 17. Runtime Verification & Testing

### Verify Containers
Run `docker ps` to see if all three containers are actively running.

### Verify Backend Health
Open a web browser and go to `http://localhost:8000/docs`. You will see the auto-generated Swagger UI demonstrating that the API is alive and ready to test.

### Test APIs natively
Use the Swagger UI (`/docs`) to manually send a fake location ping and verify the database returns a `200 OK` success response.

### Verify Database
Log into your Supabase dashboard online and check the `locations` table to see if your manual data appeared.

## 18. Common Issues & Troubleshooting

- **Maps not showing data**: Ensure your Flutter emulator is sending GPS data. In Android Studio, click the three dots (...) next to the emulator, go to "Location", and simulate a route.
- **Geo-fence not triggering**: Ensure the geofence radius in the database is small enough to trigger a breach during your simulated movement.
- **AI not detecting anomalies**: The AI needs continuous data points to calculate speed. Send multiple locations several kilometers apart within a few seconds to trigger an "impossible_speed" anomaly.
- **Login/Network Issues in Flutter**: Ensure the `_baseUrl` in Flutter is `http://10.0.2.2:8000` and NOT `localhost`, as the Android emulator has its own internal networking.

## 19. Conclusion

### Summary
The SafeNex MVP has successfully established a multi-platform architecture. By leveraging modern tools like Flutter, FastAPI, Next.js, and Supabase, the project delivers real-time tracking, SOS escalation, and a professional command center.

### Limitations & Future Ready
Currently, the system relies strictly on smartphone availability. Geo-fencing requires administrative database setup rather than simple UI drawing. However, the decoupled architecture (Dockerized API) means the system is fully prepared for future expansions, such as ESP32 hardware tracking integration (Phase 6) and complex Machine Learning model deployment.

## 20. Appendix

### Useful Commands
- `docker-compose build --no-cache` (Forces a clean rebuild of all environments)
- `docker exec -it <container_name> bash` (Enter a running container terminal)

### Sample API Request (Update Location)
```json
POST /api/v1/locations/update
{
  "user_id": "b9c3f4a1-8d2e-4c7b-9e1f-a5d6c8b2e3f4",
  "latitude": 31.5204,
  "longitude": 74.3587,
  "timestamp": "2026-03-01T10:00:00Z"
}
```

### Glossary
- **MVP**: Minimum Viable Product.
- **RLS**: Row-Level Security.
- **JWT**: JSON Web Token.
- **API**: Application Programming Interface.
